coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) from nanosecond pulsed laser ablation in ethanol
coredata.dc:creator: Yogesh, Gaurav Kumar
Shuaib, E.P.
Kalai Priya, A.
Rohini, P.
Anandhan, Sathyan Vivekanand
Krishnan, Uma Maheswari
Kalyanavalli, V.
Shukla, Shivam
Sastikumar, D.
coredata.dc:description: Traditionally, CNPs directly obtained from nanosecond pulsed laser ablation (ns-PLA) in an organic solvent like ethanol, isopropyl alcohol and acetone, possess toxic characteristics with excellent fluorescent properties. However, water-soluble CNPs are found to be non-toxic with weak fluorescent properties. In this study, a post-ablation method was utilized to achieve water-soluble fluorescent CNPs. Initially, the colloidal Eth-CNPs solution was obtained from ns-PLA of charcoal powder in the ethanol, which was subsequently dried at room temperature and then dispersed in water to achieve the final water-soluble CNPs (W-CNPs). The High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) image analysis of W-CNPs shows quasi-spherical size particle distribution with an average particle size found to be around 21 nm. XPS studies of charcoal powder and W-CNPs confirm the creation of fluorescent emissive defects state on the surface of W-CNPs under laser ablation in ethanol. Photoluminescence studies of W-CNPs demonstrated the wavelength-dependent PL emission, with peak PL emission in the blue region of the visible spectrum. In vitro studies indicated the non-toxic nature of the W-CNPs at low and high concentration (1, 5 and 10% v/v) in human cardiomyocytes. Herein, the W-CNPs obtained from post-ns-PLA was found to exhibit both blue and green fluorescence in the cardiomyocytes and hence, it can be exploited as a fluoro probe in bioimaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Charcoal
Laser Ablation
CNPs
Fluorescence
Cardiomyocytes
Cell-imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g. technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis process of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs from nanosecond laser ablation of charcoal powders in ethanol.
Snapshot images of colloidal (a) Eth-CNPs (b) water CNPs slurry, (c) W-CNPs obtained from sonication and centrifugation.
(a) HRTEM image of Eth-CNPs and (b) particle size analysis report of Eth-CNPs.
(a) HRTEM image of W-CNPs and (b) histogram plot of W-CNPs.
XRD pattern of charcoal powder, Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs.
(a, c and e) XPS survey scan of charcoal powder, partial XPS survey C 1s and O 1s scan, (b, d and f) XPS survey scan of CNPs, partial XPS survey C 1s and O 1s scan.
(a and c) UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs and (b and df) PL emission spectra of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of fluorescent W-CNPs.
Microscopic images of AC16 cells in culture with different concentration of W-CNPs after 6 h and 24 h of incubation.
AC16 cell viability at different concentrations at 6 h & 24 h. Values were presented as mean ± SD (n = 4). ** represents p ≤ 0.01 and *** represents p ≤ 0.001.
Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of AC16 cells with 1% W-CNPs.
Gaussian fitting parameter for XPS spectra of charcoal carbon and CNPs obtained from laser ablation of charcoal solution.
Size of fluorescent NCs above 10 nm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of water-soluble fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) from nanosecond pulsed laser ablation in ethanol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Traditionally, CNPs directly obtained from nanosecond pulsed laser ablation (ns-PLA) in an organic solvent like ethanol, isopropyl alcohol and acetone, possess toxic characteristics with excellent fluorescent properties. However, water-soluble CNPs are found to be non-toxic with weak fluorescent properties. In this study, a post-ablation method was utilized to achieve water-soluble fluorescent CNPs. Initially, the colloidal Eth-CNPs solution was obtained from ns-PLA of charcoal powder in the ethanol, which was subsequently dried at room temperature and then dispersed in water to achieve the final water-soluble CNPs (W-CNPs). The High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) image analysis of W-CNPs shows quasi-spherical size particle distribution with an average particle size found to be around 21 nm. XPS studies of charcoal powder and W-CNPs confirm the creation of fluorescent emissive defects state on the surface of W-CNPs under laser ablation in ethanol. Photoluminescence studies of W-CNPs demonstrated the wavelength-dependent PL emission, with peak PL emission in the blue region of the visible spectrum. In vitro studies indicated the non-toxic nature of the W-CNPs at low and high concentration (1, 5 and 10% v/v) in human cardiomyocytes. Herein, the W-CNPs obtained from post-ns-PLA was found to exhibit both blue and green fluorescence in the cardiomyocytes and hence, it can be exploited as a fluoro probe in bioimaging applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials constitute a different class of nanostructure that varies in shape, size and dimensions such as nanosheet (2D), nanotubes (1D) and fullerene (0D) [1]. However, another class of nanocarbon (NCs) were serendipitously discovered in 2004, by researchers while separating the single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) based on size by using gel-electrophoresis technique [2]. They observed that SWCNTs suspension gets separated into three distinct brands, including one of the fast-moving band of highly fluorescent nanomaterials [2]. The initial studies of fragmented nanotubes (nanosized carbonaceous material) display the size-dependent fluorescent properties. Since, then the discovery of nanocarbon materials (NCs), numerous research groups have started the investigation of their physiochemical properties, the origin of fluorescence emission, better synthesis route and novel application in various areas [1,3]. This nanocarbon has attracted a considerable amount of attention of several researchers because of easy to synthesize, chemically inert, non-toxic which have advantageous over conventional semiconductor-based quantum dots where size, cost and biocompatibility are critical [4,5].

NCs comprise mainly quasispherical nanoparticles with ranges from 1 to 100 nm [1]. These NCs were named differently such as carbon dots (CDs), carbon nanoparticles (CNPs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs), depending on the choice of carbon precursor, structural and physicochemical properties. NCs were commonly prepared by various routes such as oxidation of candle soot with nitric acid [3,6,7], hydrothermal decomposition and microwave treatment of organic compounds [4,8,9] etc. However, these conventional methods suffer from various disadvantageous such as complicated and time-consuming separation procedure and poor control of over-sizes, fluorescence quenching due to increase particles size [10,11].

Now, nanosecond pulsed laser ablation in liquid (ns-PLAL) is considered as a greener and efficient technique for the synthesis of fluorescent NCs that neither involved the separation procedure or produced these toxic by-product [10,12]. Previously, several research groups have demonstrated the synthesis of fluorescent NCs from ns-PLA in organic solvents like ethanol, dimethylformamide, acetone, isopropyl alcohol and cyclohexane [13,14,23,15–22]. These NCs possessed strong fluorescence emission but their toxicity limited for bioimaging application. So it is important to synthesize biocompatible NCs for various biological applications. NCs were obtained from different precursors like PEG, carbon cloth, coal, and have been studied for bioimaging studies in cells such as HeLa, MCF-7 and PanC-1 [24–27].

Hence, it is important to address the problem faced in conventional ns-PLA in the liquid method in obtaining the fluorescent CNPs. In this work, we try to shed a light on the effect of post-irradiation procedure of ns-PLA in an organic solvent for obtaining highly fluorescent water-soluble CNPs.

Fig. 1 shows flow-diagram of ns-PLA of charcoal powder in ethanol. The detailed design of ns-PLA in ethanol is discussed in our previous studies [28]. In this experiment, commercially available charcoal powder and ethanol (Alfa Aesar, Spectrum India) were used without any further purification. Typically, a charcoal solution of 100 mL (1 mg/mL) was prepared for laser ablation. About 25 mL of charcoal solution in 100 mL beaker was used to irradiate with unfocused beam of nanosecond (10 ns) laser pulse from Nd: YAG laser (Spectra-Physics: Model Pro 2010) with 1064 nm wavelength. The laser was operated at a pulse energy of 750 mJ for 20 min at a repetition rate of 10 Hz. During the laser irradiation, magnetic stirring was employed to expedite the movement of carbon particle and to avoid the gravitational settling. This following step was repeated 4 times for ablating the entire charcoal solution under the same experimental conditions. Nearly, 50% of ablated charcoal solution (50 mL) was used for post-ablation process and the rest of 50% used kept as for reference. At the end of laser ablation, pale yellow colloidal CNPs were obtained.

Fig. 2 a shows the yellow colour of colloidal CNPs obtained from laser ablation of charcoal powder in ethanol was named as Eth-CNPs. The colloidal Eth-CNPs was allowed to dry at room temperature for 48 h. After drying, a reddish-brown colour powder was deposited at the bottom of the beaker. A thick solution (water CNPs slurry) is obtained by adding 10 mL of double distilled water followed by sonication for 15 min (Fig. 2b). The water CNPs slurry was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 4 min to separate nanosized water disperse CNPs (W-CNPs) from bulk (Fig. 2c). The colloidal Eth-CNPs obtained from laser ablation (step-1) and W-CNPs from post-irradiation (step-3) process were both used for characterization.

The optical absorption spectra of colloidal CNPs were recorded by using a double beam spectrometer (JASCO, Model: V-670) from 200 to 700 nm. The photoluminescent studies of CNPs were carried out by using the spectrofluorometer (Horiba, Model: Fluoromax-4), and the sample was excited under various wavelengths ranging from 320 to 420 nm. The presence of different chemical compositions in the charcoal powder and CNPs were investigated by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific, Model: ESCLAB xi, MgKα 400 W, 1253.6 eV x-ray source). Wide-angle X-ray diffraction (Brucker, Model: D8 ADVANCE) of charcoal powder and thin film sample of CNPs were recoded with Cu Kα radiation λ = 1.54056 Å. High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopic (HRTEM) (JEOL 2010, Model: JSM, 200 kV) analysis of colloidal CNPs was recorded for evaluating the average particles sizes. A few drops of colloidal CNPs were placed on the carbon-coated copper grid and allowed to evaporate at room temperature before analysis.

Human cardiomyocytes (AC16) (Merck-Millipore, USA) were maintained in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium/F-12 (DMEM/F-12) (Sigma-Aldrich, USA) supplemented with 2 mM L-Glutamine (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), 12.5% fetal bovine serum (FBS) (Gibco, USA) and 1X penicillin–streptomycin (Gibco, USA) at 37 °C and incubated in 5% CO2 (Cellmate, Esco, Singapore). Tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) without the addition of the W-CNPs was used as a control. The adhered cells were treated with 1, 5 and 10% v/v of W-CNPs. The viability was tested after 6 and 24 h of incubation with W-CNPs. Cell morphology was imaged at each time point by using an inverted microscope (Eclipse TS100, Nikon, Japan) and MTS assay was carried out to quantify the viable cells. The samples were washed with DPBS (Dulbecco's phosphate-buffered saline) to remove non-adhered cells and then 100 μL of serum-free media was added. MTS reagent (20 μL) (Promega, USA) was added to each sample well and incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. The reaction was stopped by adding 10% sodium dodecyl sulphate (SDS) solution and the absorbance was recorded at 490 nm in microplate spectrophotometer (Epoch 2, Biotek, USA). To study the cell uptake of W-CNPs, 30,000 cells per well were seeded in a 6-well plate. W-CNPs (1% v/v) was added to the cultured cells, After 6 and 24 h of incubation, cells were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde and imaged under 60X oil immersion using laser scanning confocal microscope (FV1000, Olympus, Japan).

Two–way analysis of variance (two–way ANOVA) and Bonferroni post-hoc test was used to study the level of statistical significance between time points. A p-value of less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. Values were expressed as mean ± SD and the error bar represents the SD.

Fig. 3 a shows the HRTEM image of colloidal Eth-CNPs obtained after laser ablation of charcoal powder in ethanol. HRTEM image of Eth-CNPs displays a very smaller number of particles with a higher degree of agglomeration. For estimating the average particle size, the dynamic light scattering technique has been used. The particle size analysis report shows a wide distribution of particles size with the average particle is found to be around 100 nm (Fig. 3b). No sign of lattice fringes was observed in high magnification image of HRTEM (Inset Fig. 3a). Whereas W-CNPs obtained from drying and dispersion of Eth-CNPs in water clearly shows the well-dispersed particle size distribution (Fig. 4 a). For estimating the average particle size, statistical evaluation of various particles size was performed by ImageJ software. The average particle size of W-CNPs is found to be around 21 nm (Fig. 4b). Moreover, W-CNPs does not show any sign of aggregation with the average particle size is much smaller than the Eth-CNPs.
Fig. 4 shows the HRTEM image of colloidal CNPs obtained after laser ablation of charcoal in ethanol and post-irradiation process.

Fig. 5 shows the XRD pattern of carbon precursor, Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs. XRD pattern of charcoal powder exhibits the amorphous nature. The Bragg diffraction of charcoal exhibits the broad peak at around 2
θ =
24.3 and 43.4 corresponds to (002) and (100) plane of amorphous carbon structure [29], whereas small sharp peak appearing at 2
θ =
26.6 corresponding to (002) plane exhibits ordered graphitic carbon structure [30]. The XRD pattern of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs display a single broad peak centred at 2
θ =
22.6 corresponds to the typical kind of amorphous carbon nanomaterials [31].

XPS has been well known to be one of the most powerful and non-destructive technique to determine the structure of carbon materials [28,32]. Fig. 6 a-f shows the XPS survey scan of charcoal powder and W-CNPs for their chemical composition analysis. Since the carbon contains various defects such as edges, functional groups, pentagon, heptagons and sp3-carbon [33]. Hence it is important to investigate C 1s and O 1s peaks for its confirmation, respectively. Previously, XPS analysis of carbon materials summarized the differences between the sp2-C and sp3-C based on diamond and graphitic carbons. Thus general assumption was C 1s peak attributed for sp3-C (
> 284.5
e V
), is at higher binding energy than sp2-C (
284.3
e V
) (Fig. 6c) [33]. In contrary, C 1s peak position of CNPs is found to shift to slighter lower binding energy (
283.3
e V
) by
1
e V
than usual peak position for sp2-C
a t
284.4
e V
(Fig. 6d) [33–36]. However, other researchers were also observed that the peak position of sp3-C (283.3 eV) was lower than that of sp2-C (284.5 eV). Fujimoto et al. suggested the reason for the shift of C 1s peak may arise due to charging effect on carbon [33]. The origin of sp3-C peak at lower binding energy may arise due to the charging effect, which is caused by the disconnection of the pathway of electron between sp2-C and sp3-C.

XPS survey scan of both charcoal and W-CNPs display only two peaks positioned at 284 and 531 eV (Fig. 6a-b), which corresponds to the C 1s of sp2-C/sp3-C carbon and O 1s of oxygenous functional groups, respectively [37]. The partial XPS scan of C 1s peak of charcoal powder was deconvoluted into three peaks at the binding of 284.3, 285.0 and 288.9 eV corresponding to the sp2-C hybridized carbon skeleton, oxygenous functional groups (COH) and carboxylic (CO) functional groups on/within the surface of carbon particles [32,37,38] (Fig. 6c). While the Singlet O 1s peak is also deconvoluted into three peaks at the biding energies of 530.8, 532.2 and 533.0 eV, which corresponds to the oxygenous functional groups like CO, CO, OCO (Fig. 6e). The relative ratio of the atomic percentage of carbon and oxygen in charcoal is found to be around 93 and 7% respectively.

Whereas the full XPS survey scan of W-CNPs display a sharp rise in intensity of O 1s peak, which suggests passivation of carbon nanoparticles with various oxygenous functional groups under laser ablation. The partial XPS spectrum of C 1s was deconvoluted into three peaks positioned at 283.3, 284.5 and 287.0 eV corresponding to sp3-C, sp2-C and sp3-COC respectively (Fig. 6d) [33]. Whereas, singlet O 1s peak is also deconvoluted into three at the binding energies of 530.7, 531.2 and 532.0 eV corresponding to the different functional groups such as CO, CO and OCO, (Fig. 6f) respectively [33]. The relative abundance of the atomic percentage of carbon and oxygen in CNPs is 83.3 and 16.7%, respectively. Fujimoto et al. also suggested the relative increment in FWHM of C 1 s peak for sp3-C in CNPs to sp2-C in the charcoal powder were caused by the presence of electron-withdrawing groups, CH and pentagon and heptagon [33] (Table 1 ).

Fig. 7 (a and c) shows the UV–Vis absorbance spectra of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs, respectively. A typical absorbance peak of carbon nanostructure is observed at around 230–280 nm. However, the absorbance peak observed below 230 nm arises due to polyynes carbons. The characteristics absorbance peak at 215 and 225 nm in Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs corresponds to polyynes carbon structure C8H2 [39]. The absorbance peak at around 230 nm attributed to the n-
π ∗
of CC in Eth-CNPs and the shoulder peak at
250 - 300
nm corresponds to the
π
-
π ∗
of CC and CO, respectively [39,40]. During the ns-PLAL, the high energy photochemical process overcomes the polyynes moiety activation barrier in a short period, which result in the generation of short size polyynes [39–43].

Fig. 7(b-d) shows the steady-state PL emission spectra of Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs. The PL emission profile of both Eth-CNPs and W-CNPs exhibits the excitation dependent PL emission. The snapshot image of Fig. 7(b and d) shows the luminescent colloidal CNPs under UV light excitation (355 nm). Nearly all kinds of carbon nanostructure display similar kind of PL emission characteristics (excitation dependent PL) irrespective of synthesis route and carbon precursor [44]. Primarily, the excitation dependent PL emission in carbon nanostructure was explained by three ways (i) quantum confinement effect, (ii) conjugated aromatic domains and (iii) surface traps [45–49].

The quantum confinement effect directly links the size of carbon nanostructure with PL emission profile [45]. Previous reports suggested that the cut-off particle size for quantum confinement effect appearance is less than 10 nm [50–53]. However, the size distribution of CDs fluctuates significantly, and it raises above from 10 nm to a few hundred nanometers [4,39,62,54–61]. Tang et al. reported that the excitation dependent PL emission for GQDs, whereas the PL peaks of GQDs were located at the same position with different sizes 3.2, 10.7 and 21 nm [57]. Thus, the quantum size effect has confronted with a problem. To interpret this result, Sun et al. proposed that existence of nanosized conjugated aromatic domains (isolated sp2 cluster) present within the sp3 carbon matrix. Moreover, further investigation reveals the small conjugated domains with various sizes of 1–5 nm coexisted in single particles [63]. Once such aromatic domains excited with certain wavelength then only specific size nanodomain dominate the PL emission. Therefore, broader size distribution nanodomains lead to broadening of PL emission and higher fraction of larger size nanodomains cause the redshift in PL emission as well [47,64]. Another remarkable feature of PL emission in carbon nanostructure is fixed blue emission despite the different preparation technique are used. Several researchers suggest that blue emission arises from surface traps (defect state) [1,45]. At the surface of carbon nanostructures, there are an abundant amount of oxygen-related groups, like COOH, OH and COC, respectively[45]. The initial studies on CDs revealed the presence of carboxylic functional groups and most importantly, the absence of characteristics peak of C-H bending mode of hydrocarbon. Thus, the PL emission was originated from oxygenous functionalities and not from a polyaromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) [2,65]. Nguyen et. al. demonstrate that each oxygen-related groups create the energy level and become an isolated emission centre [38,66]. Each surface functional groups are likely to distributed in energy levels such as CO group at 380 nm and CO group at 430 nm [38,67]. Almost all the reports published so far correlate the surface passivation on crystalline carbon nanostructure [44,45]. In the present study, the obtained CNPs are amorphous with an average particle size (21 nm) is larger than 10 nm. Hence, the above mechanism is loosely applicable. Siddique et al. reported that amorphous carbon nanostructure contains abundant amount of empty or densely packed local atomic defects. Besides this high entropy, disordered nature of the entire structure produces multiple luminescence centres which contribute to intense PL emission. Later studies reveal the surfaces traps or surface functional groups are primarily responsible for PL emission in carbon nanostructure. The fact that both crystalline and amorphous carbon nanostructures exhibit similar PL characteristics indicating surface traps/defects plays a crucial role in PL emission [44]. Previously, several other researchers also observed similar PL characteristics for larger size particle distribution (Table 2 ).

At the end of 24 h, the fluorescence intensity was bright when compared to the 6-hour incubation. The absence of significant toxicity suggested that the particles were cytocompatible. The presence of the nanoparticles inside the cardiomyocyte after 24 h implied lysosomal escape. Earlier it was reported that the hydrophilic CNPs take up the routes of caveolin-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-dependent endocytosis and macropinocytosis in A549 cells [68] for internalization. The water dispersibility, size and the biocompatibility of the nanoparticles suggests that these W-CNPs are a promising candidate of bioimaging probe.

In this work, we report post laser irradiation method to obtain water-soluble fluorescent CNPs from laser ablation of charcoal powder in ethanol. HRTEM images and XRD analysis of W-CNPs shows the amorphous nature of carbon structure with an average particle of 21 nm. XPS analysis demonstrates that W-CNPs largely contains the sp3-C structure with small amount of sp2-C carbon as well. Detailed structural and spectroscopic studies of W-CNPs suggest that strong fluorescence emission originated from surface functional groups rather than carbogenic core. The PL emission spectra of W-CNPs in water shows the intense PL emission in bluish (445 nm) region of the visible spectrum at the excitation wavelength of 380 nm. In vitro studies demonstrated the cytocompatibility of W-CNPs in human cardiomyocytes without any significant reduction in cellular viability even at concentrations of 10%. Internalization of the W-CNPs was seen within 6 h without affecting the PL emission indicating its photostability in the biological milieu. Overall, our results indicate that the post laser ablation technique is a simple and reliable method for the preparation of fluorescent non-toxic W-CNPs that can be explored further for biomedical applications.

Gaurav Kumar Yogesh: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. E.P. Shuaib: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology. A. Kalai Priya: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis. P. Rohini: Data curation, Formal analysis. Sathyan Vivekanand Anandhan: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Uma Maheswari Krishnan: Conceptualization, Resources, Software, Writing - review & editing. V. Kalyanvalli: Data curation, Formal analysis. Shivam Shukla: Data curation, Formal analysis. D Sastikumar: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: All persons who have made substantial contributions to the work reported in the manuscript (e.g., technical help, writing and editing assistance, general support).
